description: Disposal marks the surviving dependent cone dirty but MUST NOT run the effects it reaches, then or later (#lzspecedgeindex). Disposal is not a publish. An effect reached by the teardown walk is a *survivor* — it depended on the disposed node and is still live — so running it re-enters a compute that reads the node currently being torn down. In a binding that flushes effects inside `dispose`, that turns disposal itself into an error and breaks teardown idempotence, with scope teardown the first casualty since it disposes members in sequence. In a binding that defers its flush the failure is quieter but still wrong: the effect sits queued and fires later, during some unrelated flush, as a spurious rerun the caller never triggered. This fixture catches BOTH shapes, which is why it does not stop at the disposal step. Step 6 asserts no effect ran during the dispose — that catches the flush-inside-disposal binding. Step 7 then writes an UNRELATED cell and asserts only that cell's own effect ran — that catches the deferred binding, because a spuriously scheduled `watch` would fire on this flush. The contract for the survivor is 'errors on next recompute', not 'errors immediately', so `watch` remains live and subscribed; it simply must not be run by the teardown. Known to be invisible to the rest of this corpus: lazily-dart and lazily-cpp each mutated their implementation to schedule effects during disposal and all nine other reactive-graph fixtures stayed green, because every one of them disposes an effect in the cone rather than leaving a survivor.

expect.note: watch is a live dependent of mid; keeper is independent and exists only to give step 7 something to flush
the first discriminating assertion. watch is in the disposed cone and is NOT itself disposed, so a binding that flushes effects inside dispose runs it here and reports ['watch'].

the second discriminating assertion, and the one a deferred-flush binding needs. If disposal scheduled watch instead of only marking it, watch fires on this unrelated flush as a spurious rerun and appears here alongside keeper.
